coredata.dc:title: Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Yongqiang
Shao, Jingwei
Chen, Congqiang
Li, Hao
Wang, Ruixue
Chi, Yuwu
Lin, Xiaomei
Chen, Guonan
coredata.dc:description: An easy bottom–up method for the preparation of photoluminescent (PL) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene oxide (GO) has been developed by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solutions. The GQDs are nanosheets ∼15nm in width, and 0.5–2.0nm in thickness. They show a relatively strong (9.0%) PL quantum yield and an excitation-independent PL emission activity. In contrast, the GO nanostructures consist of sheets that are hundreds of nanometers in width and ∼1nm in height. They exhibit a relatively weak (2.2%) PL quantum yield and an excitation-dependent PL emission activity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the the synthesis of GQDs and GO. The black dots in the GO represent oxygen atoms.
XRD pattern for the GQDs (upper line) and GO (lower line).
FTIR spectra of CA (a), GQDs (b) and GO (c).
AFM (a and c) and TEM (b and d) images of GQDs (a and b) and GO (c and d).

UV–Vis absorption of CA and the GQDs, and PL spectra of the GQDs. Inset: (upper) Emission spectra of the GQDs with excitation of different wavelength; (lower) Photographs of the solution of GQDs taken under visible light (left) and under 365nm UV light (right).

UV–Vis absorption spectra and emission spectra of the GO with excitation of different wavelength. Inset: (upper) Normalized emission spectra; (lower) Photographs of the solution of GQDs taken under visible light (left) and under 365nm UV light (right).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of the GO before (a) and after (b) reduced by 0.1mol/L NaBH4.
Emission spectra of the GO before (a) and after (b) reduced by 0.1mol/L NaBH4. The spectra were recorded progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 300 to 440nm in 20nm increments.
Elemental analysis result of CA, the obtained GQDs and GO.

head.ce:title: Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, and Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou Univiersity, Fujian 350108, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An easy bottom–up method for the preparation of photoluminescent (PL) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene oxide (GO) has been developed by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solutions. The GQDs are nanosheets ∼15nm in width, and 0.5–2.0nm in thickness. They show a relatively strong (9.0%) PL quantum yield and an excitation-independent PL emission activity. In contrast, the GO nanostructures consist of sheets that are hundreds of nanometers in width and ∼1nm in height. They exhibit a relatively weak (2.2%) PL quantum yield and an excitation-dependent PL emission activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon based luminescent nanomaterials (CLNMs), including carbon nanotube (CNT) fragments [1], surface-functionalized CNTs [2], nanodiamonds [3], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [4], have received intensive attention, due to their unique properties, such as low toxicity, high luminescent, robust chemical inertness and easy preparation. More recently, another two emerging luminescent carbon nanomaterials, graphene oxide (GO) [5], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [6], are also attracting more and more attention. GO is an atomically thin sheet of graphite that covalently decorated with oxygen-containing functional groups, either on the basal plane or at the edges. GQDs are graphene sheets smaller than 100nm [7], which present unique optical and electronic properties due to their quantum confinement and edge effects. Generally, GQDs have much smaller size and exhibit much stronger photoluminescent activity when compared with GO. However, recent researches show that GODs and GO may have the same luminescence origin, i.e. their luminescence may be related to the isolated sp2 clusters [6]. Both GO and GQDs are proposed to be applicable in many fields, such as photovoltaic devices [8], cellular imaging [9], and drug delivery [9,10]. Accordingly, developing effective strategies to prepare GO and GQDs is of significance. To date, GO is mainly prepared by chemically exfoliating graphite. As for the GQDs, more and more preparing methods, both “top–down” and “bottom–up”, have been developed. However, most “top–down” methods, including carving graphite crystallites using high-resolution electron-beam lithography [11], cutting GO via hydrothermal [12,13], reoxidation [14], or electrochemical routes [15], chemical oxidation treating carbon fibers and carbon black [16,17], cage-opening the fullerene on ruthenium surfaces [18], usually suffer from some disadvantages, such as the requirement of special equipment, low yield, critical synthesis conditions, or difficulty in controlling the size distribution of products. Those “bottom–up” strategies, namely carbonizing some special organic precursors by thermal treatment [8,19], usually allow precise control over the morphology and the size distribution of the products. However, those special organic precursors used in these methods always involve boring synthesis procedures, and are difficult to be obtained. Herein, we report a simple bottom–up method to selectively prepare GQDs and GO by tuning carbonization degree of a common organic precursor, citric acid (CA). Both the obtained GQDs and GO are blue photoluminescent (PL).
Citric acid was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Other chemicals were analytically pure and used as received.

The GQDs and GO were prepared by directly pyrolyzing CA (see Fig. 1 ). In a typical procedure of GQDs preparation, 2g CA was put into a 5mL beaker and heated to 200°C using a heating mantle. About 5min later, the CA was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange in 30min, implying the formation of GQDs. If the heating was kept on, the orange liquid would finally turn to black solid in about 2h, suggesting the formation of GO. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 100mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH, the aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained. The black solid was dissolved with 50mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution, and further neutralized with the same concentration of NaOH, resulting the aqueous solution of GO.

Elemental analysis was carried out using an organic elemental analyzer (Vario MICRO). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by a Japan Rigaku D/max-3C using Cu Ka radiation. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by tapping-mode on a Nanoscope IIIa (Digital Instruments) with NSC15 tips (silicon cantilever, MikroMasch). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) observations were made on a Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200KV electron microscope. UV–Vis absorption was characterized by a UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750). All FL spectra were obtained by a FL spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian).

An easy and low cost bottom–up method has been developed to prepare fluorescent GQDs and GO by tuning the carbonization degree of CA. After moderate pyrolysis, CA can be partially carbonized to form nanosheets (GQDs) containing abundant small sp2 clusters that isolated within the incompletely carbonized CA. The sp2 clusters are uniform in size and well passivated by the incompletely carbonized CA. Therefore, the GQDs show a strong and excitation-independent PL activity. After full pyrolysis, CA is almost completely carbonized, forming larger nanosheets (i.e. GO). The GO contain abundant small sp2 clusters isolated within the sp3 C–O matrix. Those sp2 clusters should be non-uniform in size and poorly passivated, resulting in a weak and excitation-dependent PL activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21075018), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Chinese University (NCET-10-0019), the Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar of Fujian Province, China (2009J06003), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT1116).
